stops.name: Kiyomizu-dera
stops.shortDescription: A spectacular wooden temple founded in 778, famous for its massive stage jutting out over the hillside. Remarkably, not a single nail was used in the construction of its main hall.

stops.name: Sannenzaka
stops.shortDescription: A beautifully preserved, stone-paved pedestrian street lined with traditional wooden machiya houses. Legend says that if you fall on these sloping steps, you will face three years of bad luck.

stops.name: Hokan-ji Temple (Yasaka Pagoda)
stops.shortDescription: This elegant five-story pagoda is the last remaining structure of a 6th-century temple complex. It stands at 46 meters tall and serves as the most iconic visual landmark of the Higashiyama district.

stops.name: Kodai-ji Temple
stops.shortDescription: Established in 1606 by the widow of Toyotomi Hideyoshi to pray for her late husband. It features exquisite Zen gardens, richly decorated interiors, and a serene bamboo grove.

stops.name: Maruyama Park
stops.shortDescription: Kyoto's oldest public park, renowned as the city's premier destination for cherry blossom viewing. The centerpiece is a massive, centuries-old weeping cherry tree that is illuminated at night.

stops.name: Yasaka Shrine
stops.shortDescription: Once known as Gion Shrine, this vibrant Shinto sanctuary was founded over 1,350 years ago. It is famous for hosting the Gion Matsuri, one of Japan's largest and most famous summer festivals.

stops.name: Gion Hanamikoji Street
stops.shortDescription: The main street of Kyoto's most famous geisha district. Lined with exclusive ochaya (teahouses), you might be lucky enough to spot a geiko or maiko hurrying to an evening appointment.

stops.name: Tatsumi Bridge (Gion Shirakawa)
stops.shortDescription: A small, incredibly photogenic stone bridge crossing the Shirakawa Canal. Surrounded by weeping willows and traditional wooden inns, it perfectly epitomizes the classic beauty of historic Kyoto.

stops.name: Chion-in Temple
stops.shortDescription: The head temple of the Jodo sect of Buddhism. Its massive, awe-inspiring Sanmon gate is the largest wooden gate in Japan, standing an imposing 24 meters tall.

stops.name: Shoren-in Temple
stops.shortDescription: A tranquil Monzeki temple, meaning its head priests were historically members of the imperial family. It features stunning landscaped gardens and ancient, giant camphor trees near the entrance.

stops.name: Heian Shrine
stops.shortDescription: Built in 1895 to commemorate the 1,100th anniversary of Kyoto's founding as the capital. It features a massive, iconic vermilion torii gate and expansive, beautiful strolling pond gardens.

stops.name: Okazaki Shrine
stops.shortDescription: Affectionately known as the 'Rabbit Shrine,' dedicated to fertility and safe childbirth. The peaceful temple grounds are delightfully covered in hundreds of adorable rabbit statues.

stops.name: Nanzen-ji Temple
stops.shortDescription: One of the most important Zen temples in all of Japan. Its sprawling forested grounds contain beautifully austere rock gardens and a large, unexpected Roman-style brick aqueduct.

stops.name: Philosopher's Path
stops.shortDescription: A highly scenic, two-kilometer stone path following a canal lined with hundreds of cherry trees. It was named after a famous Kyoto University philosophy professor who walked it daily.
